topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias stood at the edge of the clearing, his shadow stretching long and thin under the late afternoon sun. Where a young sapling, planted with painstaking care during a summer vacation, had stood for nearly a decade, there was now only a ragged scar on the earth. He knelt, brushing away the damp soil, his fingers tracing the uneven cut where the trunk had once been. A strange hollowness settled in his chest, a dull ache that made his breath catch in his throat. He looked around the surrounding woods, the familiar canopy suddenly feeling alien and indifferent. "That's impossible," he murmured, the sound thin and reedy. He ran a hand over the exposed roots, searching for any sign of struggle, any evidence that this wasn't just a natural event. His jaw tightened, and he stood up abruptly, turning to face the distant, silent line of trees, his eyes wide and unfocused.

[story 2]

The scent of pine and damp earth hit Clara as she walked toward the spot. She stopped dead when she saw it—a stark, freshly cleared patch where her childhood endeavor used to thrive. It was a gaping wound in the landscape. Clara slowly lowered herself to her knees, her movements stiff, almost hesitant. She poked at the bare ground with the toe of her worn boot, then looked up at the towering, unperturbed oaks that had watched her growth. A tightness began to build behind her ribs, a sensation like a knot tightening with unspoken questions. "No," she whispered, the word barely audible above the rustle of leaves. She stood up again, taking a shaky step back, her gaze darting between the stump and the surrounding wilderness, her hands clenching and unclenching at her sides.

[story 3]

Marcus approached the site with a slow, deliberate gait. He stopped, his shoulders slumping slightly as he took in the scene. The space where his youthful ambition had taken root was now just disturbed earth, a raw, exposed wound. He crouched down, his gaze sweeping over the severed remnants of the young tree. A tremor ran through his hands as he touched the rough bark that remained, feeling the unnatural flatness of the cut. He didn't speak immediately; instead, he just stared, his brow furrowed deeply, his breathing becoming shallow and quick. He rose slowly, backing away a few paces, shaking his head as if trying to dislodge a persistent, unwelcome thought.
